questions.scenario: A teammate searches all logs for the word `error` and finds too much noise. The incident has operation ID `checkout-5001` and started around 09:42 UTC. What makes the search more useful?

options.text: Target the relevant table and filter by operation ID or time window before reading individual messages.
Search every table without filters so no possible log line is missed.
Ignore logs and only use tags, because tags are easier to read during incidents.
Delete old tables so the workspace has fewer rows to search.

questions.explanation: KQL is useful because it narrows evidence by table, time, operation, status, or resource. A broad word search is usually too noisy for incident work.

questions.scenario: The workspace keeps logs for 30 days. Support asks for a request from six months ago, but the order record still exists in SQL. What should the team explain?

options.text: The database record and observability evidence have different retention rules, so the team may still have business data without the old logs.
The logs must exist somewhere because Azure keeps every workspace table forever by default.
The request can be recreated by changing the operation ID in a query.
The action group should be updated because notification routing controls historical log retention.

questions.explanation: Application data and observability data are retained for different reasons and different durations. The team should set retention based on support, cost, and compliance needs.
